metadata.title: Lepidocyrtus (Cinctocyrtus) bobwoodruffi Soto-Adames, Jenks and Daly 2025, new species
metadata.description:
Lepidocyrtus (Cinctocyrtus) bobwoodruffi Soto-Adames, Jenks and Daly, new species

(Fig. 1A, 2A–C, 4–5)

Description. SLide Mounted sPeciMens uP to 1.2 MM Long, excLuding antennae and fuRcuLa. BackgRound coLoR (Fig. 2A–C) white, Ant. 1 white, Ant. 2-4 PuRPLish; ABd. 4 with two shoRt MediaLLy unconnected tRansveRsaL Bands (Fig. 2A–B) that often aPPeaR as two anteRoLateRaL daRk PuRPLe sPots (Fig. 2C); Legs, coLLoPhoRe and fuRcuLa white. ScaLes aBsent fRoM antennae, Legs, coLLoPhoRe and doRsaL face of ManuBRiuM. MacRosetae foRMuLa 00/00/0132+11; functionaL foRMuLa 00/0/12+11

Head. Antennae 1.5×as Long as head. APicaL PaPiLLa on Ant. 4 aBsent; suBaPicaL sensiLLuM caPitate. Sense oRgan on Ant. 3 in the foRM of two exPosed shoRt Rods. Eyes G and H (Fig. 4A) suBequaL and sMaLLeR than C. Eye Patch with thRee oceLLaR setae (Fig. 4B): s sMooth; t ciLiate and LongeR than s; p ciLiate and LongeR than t. Head doRsaLLy without outstanding MediaL PaiRed MacRosetae (Fig. 4A), MacRoseta A0 PResent, A1 ciLiate, aLL setae anteRioR to A0 fusifoRM and heaviLy ciLiate, aLL MediaL setae PosteRioR to, and incLuding A2, sMooth and taPeRed. PRe-LaBRaL setae ciLiate. ALL LaBRaL setae sMooth; LaBRaL PaPiLLae sMooth. OuteR MaxiLLaRy LoBe with BasaL seta sMooth, LongeR than distaL aPPendage; suBLoBaL PLate with fouR seta-Like PRocesses. LateRaL aPPendage of LaBiaL PaPiLLa E (LP in Fig. 4C) BLunt, shoRt, BaReLy Reaching haLf distance to tiP of PaPiLLa. LaBiuM PRoxiMaL setae sMooth. LaBiaL chaetotaxy as A1-5, M2ôEL1L2 (Fig. 4D): r Reduced, sMooth, conic, soMetiMes aBsent; L2 cLeaRLy LongeR than L1. ALL Post-LaBiaL setae ciLiate (Fig. 4D): 5+5 Mc aLong cePhaLic gRoove; fiRst Post-LaBiaL Row with setae C1, E1, L1, and two LateRaL conic setae; seta C2i PResent; setae C2, E2 and L2 aRRanged as an inveRted ‘L’, with E2 cLeaRLy anteRioR to C2 and L2 (Fig. 4D).

Body. MesothoRacic hood weLL deveLoPed, Rounded, Pushing head down (Fig. 2A, B); coLLaR MediaLLy with a singLe Row of BRoadLy acuMinate MacRosetae as in L. brambilae new sPecies (Fig. 6H); PosteRioR MacRosetae aBsent; MediaL and PosteRioR fieLds oLigochaetotic. MetathoRax without MacRosetae. FiRst aBdoMinaL segMent (Fig. 4E) with eLeven PosteRioR setae; seta a6 PResent. Second aBdoMinaL segMent (Fig. 4F) with aLL suPPLeMentaRy setae (a2i, Li, Lm, Ll) ciLiate fusifoRM, cLuBBed oR fan-shaPed; a2 cLuBBed; a2p ciLiate, shoRteR than p4; a3 sMooth, inseRted anteRioR to a2i, not Reaching a2 oR as; as Minute, Less than haLf Length of a2 oR m3e; m3 a shoRt, ciLiate seta suBequaL to sLightLy LongeR than p4 But with socket Modified as a ReguLaR Mc; m3e, p4, m4 and p5 sMooth; p5 LongeR than p4; m5 a PaRaLLeL-sided MacRoseta; a6 fusifoRM; m4i and p5p aBsent. ThiRd aBdoMinaL segMent (Fig. 4G) doRsaLLy with aLL suPPLeMentaRy setae (a2i, a2ii, Li, Lm, Ll, im, em) fusifoRM oR fan-shaPed; a2 cLuBBed; a3 sMooth, weLL anteRioR to and not Reaching as; as shoRt, Less than haLf m3; m3 not Reaching p3; a6 and am6 fusifoRM; a7 ciLiate, soMetiMes fusifoRM, anteRioR to a5 and not Reaching am6; MacRoseta m7 PResent; MicRo-sensiLLuM d2 PResent; c1 aBsent. FouRth aBdoMinaL segMent (Fig. 4H, 5B) doRsaLLy with two inneR MacRosetae (B5, B6), Both Long and gRaduaLLy taPeRed; Sm a MicRoseta. BothRiotRichaL coMPLex on ABd. 4 (Fig. 5A) with aLL setae ciLiate, fusifoRM oR fan-shaPed; seta s aBsent; setae a, m and D1 suBequaL; setae C1p, T3 and D1p sMooth and suBequaL, T3 sLightLy anteRioR and oveRLaPPing D1p; D1p not Reaching Pe oR Pi. DetaiL of LateRaL chaetotaxy as in FiguRe 4H, 5B; PRiMaRy eLeMents as T6, D2, D3, E1, E2, E3, E4, F1, F2, F3; Mc F2 inseRted PosteRioR to E2. LateRaL MaRgin of ABd. 4 usuaLLy with seven (Range 3–9) PseudoPoRes. PosteRioR MaRgin of ABd. 4 with 11+11 setae.

Appendages. TRochanteRaL oRgan (Fig. 5C) V-shaPed, with uP to 19 setae. Tenet haiR sPatuLate, Long, aBout 1.77×as Long as unguicuLus. PosteRioR sMooth seta shoRt, aBout haLf as Long (0.54×) as tenent haiR and suBequaL (0.96×) to unguicuLus. Unguis (Fig. 5D) with fouR sMaLL wedge-shaPed inneR teeth; BT and PUT suBequaL, DUT Minute, soMetiMes aBsent; teeth sPRead as 45, 45, 65, 83, BT not Reaching PUT. UnguicuLus LanceoLate to soMewhat swoLLen BasaLLy, usuaLLy sMooth, soMetiMes with a Minute PosteRioR tooth. CoLLoPhoRe with Many ciLiate setae on anteRioR face; vesicLe coveRs with five sMooth and seven to nine ciLiate setae; PosteRioR face (Fig. 5E) distaLLy with 1+1 sMooth MediaL setae and 3-4 + 3-4 LateRaL ciLiate setae, BasaL section of PosteRioR face with uP to 12 ciLiate setae. DistaL ManuBRiaL PLate with two PseudoPoRes and thRee fusifoRM, coaRseLy ciLiate setae. Dens tuBeRcLe (Fig. 5F) PRojecting, BLunt. MucRo (Fig. 5G) with sMooth BasaL sPine and suBequaL teeth.

Distribution. FLoRida (Fig. 1A): ALachua, CitRus and VoLusia counties.

Material examined. HOLOTYPE, feMaLe, sLide-Mounted USA, FLORIDA, Alachua Co., GainesviLLe, CLeaR Lake NatuRe PaRk, 29.6521, -82.3974, Leaf LitteR, 7.v.2020, F. Soto, Leg.: 20fsa118, FSCA 00117170. PARATYPES, saMe coLLection infoRMation as hoLotyPe, 8 sPeciMens on sLides FSCA 00117172-00117179 and 33 in aLcohoL, PaRatyPes FSCA 00117178-00117179 aRe dePosited at INHS Citrus Co., HoMosassa SPRings, 28.79964, -82.58673, Leaf LitteR easteRn side PaRking Lot, 19.xi.2018, F. Soto, J. BRaMBiLa & J. MaRceLino, Leg. 18fsa36, 1 on sLide (FSCA 00117171), 17 in aLcohoL; Volusia Co., Lake WoodRuff NationaL WiLdLife Refuge, Live Oak TRaiL, 29.1053, -81.3710, dRy Mixed haRdwood & conifeR Leaf LitteR: 24.v.2020: F. Soto & J. BRaMBiLa, Leg.: 20fsa125, 3 in aLcohoL.

Etymology. The ePithet honoRs RoBeRt WoodRuff, foR his iMPoRtant RoLe in the exPansion of the FLoRida State CoLLection of ARthRoPods and as a co-foundeR of the CenteR foR SysteMatic EntoMoLogy and Insecta Mundi (SkeLLey and KeLLeR 2022).

Remarks. Lepidocyrtus bobwoodruffi new species is the onLy New WoRLd sPecies with functionaL foRMuLa 00/0/12+11, PostLaBiaL seta C2i, ABd. 4 with MoRe than 10 PosteRioR setae, LanceoLate unguicuLus, a weLL-deveLoPed Rounded MesothoRacic hood and BLunt dentaL tuBeRcLe.AMong sPecies cuRRentLy known fRoM FLoRida, L. bobwoodruffi new species is the onLy white foRM with LateRaL sPots on ABd.4, a Long, sLendeR Body and a weLL-deveLoPed MesothoRacic hood.

In chaetotaxy, the new sPecies is Most siMiLaR to Lepidocyrtus distinctus MaRi Mutt, L. floridensis and L. marimutti ThiBaud and Najt. Lepidocyrtus distinctus diffeRs fRoM L. bobwoodruffi new species in coLoR PatteRn (L. distinctus has evenLy BLue Th. 2-ABd. 3; the new sPecies is aLMost evenLy white) caRRying an aPicaL BuLB on Ant. 4 (aBsent in L. bobwoodruffi new species), having fouR oceLLaR setae (thRee in L. bobwoodruffi new species), Lacking ABd. 2 seta a2p (PResent in L. bobwoodruffi new species), Lacking ABd. 2 seta m3, and By caRRying Mc B4 on ABd. 4 (Mc B4 aBsent in L. bobwoodruffi new species). Lepidocyrtus floridensis diffeRs fRoM the new sPecies in coLoR PatteRn and Body shaPe (cf. Fig 2A and 2G), in nuMBeR of PosteRioR setae on ABd. 4 (6-7+ 6-7 in L. floridensis and 11+ 11 in the new sPecies), Post-LaBiaL chaetotaxy (setae C2i PResent in the new sPecies and aBsent in L. floridensis), in the nuMBeR of suBLoBaL aPPendages on the OML (2 in L. floridensis, 4 in L. bobwoodruffi new species) and in the nuMBeR of LaRge LateRaL Mc on ABd. 4 (five in L. bobwoodruffi new species, thRee in L. floridensis). FoLLowing ThiBaud and Najt (1987), L. marimutti diffeRs in Lacking ABd. 2 Mc m5 (Mc m5 PResent in L. bobwoodruffi new species), in having thRee inneR MacRoseta on ABd. 4 (two in L. bobwoodruffi new species) and in having two daRk BLue LongitudinaL Bands on ABd. 4 (1+1 anteRioR sPots oR RectangLes in L. bobwoodruffi new species).

AMong NeaRctic taxa, the new sPecies ReseMBLes Lepidocyrtus unifasciatus JaMes, But accoRding to ChRistiansen and BeLLingeR (1998), this sPecies has scaLes on the antennae and feMoRa (aBsent in the new sPecies), doRsaL head MacRosetae A2, A3 and Pa5 (aLL aBsent in the new sPecies), fouR inneR MacRosetae on ABd. 4 (two in L. bobwoodruffi new species) and Lacks dentaL tuBeRcLe (PResent in the new sPecies). Lepidocyrtus finus ChRistiansen and BeLLingeR is aLso siMiLaR to the new sPecies, But ChRistiansen and BeLLingeR (1998) RePoRt that it caRRies an aPicaL BuLB on Ant. 4 (aBsent in L. bobwoodruffi new species), LaBiaL tRiangLe with two setae M (one in the new sPecies) and ABd. 4 seta D1p Much LongeR than T3 (setae suBequaL in the new sPecies).

TaBLe 1 PRovides a List of the chaRacteRs usefuL to sePaRate the nine sPecies of New WoRLd Lepidocyrtus Lacking PaiRed MediaL MacRosetae on the head, having LanceoLate unguicuLus on aLL Legs and caRRying a weLL-deveLoPed dentaL tuBeRcLe.

metadata.title: Melanochlamys radwanskii
metadata.description:
Melanochlamys radwanskii (Bałuk, 2018)

Figs 50A 1 –A 2

* Chelidonura radwanskii sp. nov. — Bałuk 2018: 500, text-figs 2B1–2B2 (only) [non text-figs 2A1–2A2 = Chelidonura piserai sp. nov.].

Type material. Holotype, MWG UW ZI/93/1252, SL: 2.8 mm, MD: 2.2 mm, Badenian, Korytnica (Poland), illustrated in Bałuk (2018: text-fig. 2B), Figs 50A 1 –A 2.

Description. “ Protoconch very small, several microns in cross-section, with oval knob shape. Remaining part of shell composed of 1.5 strongly bent shield, straightening gradually towards front, becoming almost flat and much thinner. Also present wing increasing in size towards the posterior. Distinct thickening, in form of ridge running along posterior margin till end of wing, visible both on inner and outer side. Growth lines visible on external surface; internal surface smooth.” (Bałuk 2018: 500).

Discussion. Only two Aglajidae species have been described from the Paratethys prior to our revision: Chelidonura radwanskii Bałuk, 2018, from the Badenian of the Central Paratethys Sea, and Chelidonura jolkii (Anistratenko in Anistratenko & Prisyazhnyuk, 1996) from the Sarmatian of the Eastern Paratethys Sea (Ukraine). These species were placed by Guzhov (2023) in Chelidonura A. Adams, 1850, whereas Chaban & Martynov (2000) placed the Eastern Paratethyan species in Melanochlamys Cheeseman, 1881. For the Central Paratethys species Chelidonura radwanskii, we recognize that Bałuk (2018) illustrated two specimens representing two different genera. The holotype of Chelidonura radwanskii is a Melanochlamys, characterized by a very prominent crest along the wing, whereas the paratype of Chelidonura radwanskii is indeed that genus. Melanochlamys radwanskii is reminiscent of Melanochlamys cylindrica as discussed by Ortea et al. (2024) but differs in its even more prominent crest and weak concavity adjoining the ridge abapically. Chelidonura jolkii is exceptionally similar to Genus 3 of Ortea et al. (2024: fig. 6), which nests within the Chelidonura clade. Therefore, we follow Guzhov (2023) for the Eastern Paratethyan species.

Paleoenvironment. Melanochlamys species live on sandy-muddy bottoms on the littoral zone (Montety et al. 2024).

Distribution in Central Paratethys. Badenian (Middle Miocene): Korytnica Basin: Korytnica (Poland) (Bałuk 2018).

metadata.title: Algebraic Bias Certificates for AI Systems via Zero-Point Lock and Spectral Gap Neutrality Conditions
metadata.description: The Zero-Point Lock |Σₖ exp(2πik/n)|=0 is a rigorous algebraic bias-freedom certificate for AI decision systems. When satisfied, no systematic directional preference exists — maps to demographic parity in AI fairness. Spectral gap λ₁=22/21 bounds worst-case unfairness amplification. Gromov boundary ∂H³ defines an alignment boundary — decisions interior to hyperboloid are controllable. KAM stability certificates provide interpretability: KAM-stable AI behavior is near-periodic and auditable. Implemented live in x/aigov module of omegagenesis-1 with constitutional law enforcement. κ=1.60225455 from ORCID 0009-0003-2911-444X. ZKP commitment block 40840.

data.title: Algebraic Bias Certificates for AI Systems via Zero-Point Lock and Spectral Gap Neutrality Conditions

metadata.title: H³ Hyperbolic Credit Graph Distance and Zero-Point Neutrality Certificates for Financial Networks
metadata.description: OmegaFlow v13 applies H³ hyperbolic geometry to financial network analysis. Credit networks embed with low distortion into H³(κ²). Geodesic distances d_H³(p,q)=(1/κ)·arccosh(-κ²·⟨p,q⟩_Mink) provide contagion propagation metrics superior to Euclidean distance for scale-free networks. The Zero-Point Lock |Σₖ exp(2πik/231)|=0 provides algebraically exact neutrality certificates for credit scoring. KAM stability gcd(231,260)=gcd(231,37)=gcd(260,37)=1 gates against chaotic credit regimes. Live integration via omegagenesis-1 L1 chain with UCC Article 12 CER asset class. κ=1.60225455 from ORCID 0009-0003-2911-444X. PFMI ZKP commitment block 40840.

data.title: H³ Hyperbolic Credit Graph Distance and Zero-Point Neutrality Certificates for Financial Networks

metadata.title: The Phantasic Profile of a Language
metadata.description: The phantasic profile of a language community, the statistical distribution of voluntary mental-imagery and multimodal-simulation capacity across its speakers, is a load-bearing variable in what the community's vocabulary means. A word produced and prestiged within a community skewed toward hyperphantasic capacity addresses a thicker multimodal semantic region than the same word would address in a community skewed toward aphantasic capacity. Cross-linguistic semantic analysis that ignores this variable systematically misreads imagistic, somatic, aesthetic, and contemplative vocabulary, by assuming reader-side activation capacity is uniform across language communities when it demonstrably is not. The paper lifts an established finding in cognitive science (individual phantasic variation, Zeman et al. 2015) to the community level, argues that phantasic profile is recoverable through inference from literary corpus, contemplative-tradition evidence, and contemporary cross-linguistic data, and proposes that phantasic-profile inference become a standard component of contested-term translation. Worked examples from Buddhist contemplative vocabulary, Old Norse kennings, Japanese aesthetic terms, Hebrew theological vocabulary, and the converse case of scientific-technical English as deliberately aphantasic-compatible lingua franca develop the apparatus across distinct phantasic regimes.
references: Berlin, Brent and Paul Kay. (1969). Basic Color Terms: Their Universality and Evolution. Berkeley: University of California Press.
Beyer, Stephan. (1973). The Cult of Tārā: Magic and Ritual in Tibet. Berkeley: University of California Press.
Boroditsky, Lera. (2001). Does language shape thought? Mandarin and English speakers' conceptions of time. Cognitive Psychology 43 (1): 1 to 22.
Boroditsky, Lera. (2011). How language shapes thought. Scientific American 304 (2): 62 to 65.
Dance, Carla J., Adam Ipser, and Julia Simner. (2022). The prevalence of aphantasia (imagery weakness) in the general population. Consciousness and Cognition 97: 103243.

Dance, Carla J., Marta Hodgekins, and Julia Simner. (2024). What is it like to be an aphantasic? Reflections on imagery weakness in everyday cognition. British Journal of Psychology 115 (1): 78 to 101.
Holmes, Emily A. and Andrew Mathews. (2010). Mental imagery in emotion and emotional disorders. Clinical Psychology Review 30 (3): 349 to 362.
Kapstein, Matthew T., ed. (2004). The Presence of Light: Divine Radiance and Religious Experience. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.
Kay, Paul and Terry Regier. (2003). Resolving the question of color naming universals. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 100 (15): 9085 to 9089.
Lakoff, George and Mark Johnson. (1980). Metaphors We Live By. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.
Lakoff, George and Mark Johnson. (1999). Philosophy in the Flesh: The Embodied Mind and Its Challenge to Western Thought. New York: Basic Books.
Levenson, Jon D. (1985). Sinai and Zion: An Entry into the Jewish Bible. San Francisco: HarperCollins.

data.title: The Phantasic Profile of a Language

metadata.title: Natoma: A Deterministic Authorization and Synchronization Proxy Framework for Agentic Data Clouds
metadata.description: As enterprise data infrastructures pivot toward autonomous agentic architectures, Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated as operational nodes capable of dynamically executing tools, invoking APIs, and writing to database catalogs via protocols such as the Model Context Protocol (MCP). However, these architectures introduce a severe security flaw: probabilistic LLM reasoning is fundamentally incapable of enforcing deterministic access control boundaries. This structural vulnerability leads to semantic jailbreaks, database row-level security bypass via owner's rights execution, and distributed resource exhaustion. This paper introduces the Natoma Proxy Framework, an identity-aware, rule-based interdiction gateway positioned between untrusted probabilistic agents and deterministic data runtimes. The framework combines JSON-RPC path canonicalization, cosine-similarity sliding-window semantic chunking, dynamic Personal Identifiable Information (PII) redaction for regulatory compliance, vector pre-filtering to prevent privilege escalation, and a Three-Phase Commit (3PC) protocol to suppress concurrency retry storms. We mathematically model the safety limits of vector searches, establish boundary convergence for semantic loss under hardware constraints, analyze retry phase-locking dynamics using the Kuramoto oscillator model, and evaluate egress cost optimizations. Live evaluations demonstrate that the Natoma framework guarantees zero data leakage and reduces multi-cloud egress costs by 25.52%.

data.title: Natoma: A Deterministic Authorization and Synchronization Proxy Framework for Agentic Data Clouds

metadata.title: Resolution Drift
metadata.description: The standard model of linguistic communication treats meaning as a point that survives the channel: the speaker selects a meaning, encodes it in surface form, and the hearer decodes the surface back to the meaning, with miscommunication modelled as channel noise corrupting an otherwise determinate signal. This paper argues that the model omits a transmitted parameter it cannot do without. Speakers do not only transmit meanings; they transmit, and intend to transmit, a resolution: how collapsed the content is meant to be, ranging from a sharp point to a deliberately open distribution over candidate readings. Resolution is part of the message, it is transmitted lossily and asymmetrically, and the gap between the resolution a sender intends and the resolution a receiver reconstructs is a distinct and diagnosable failure mode that the encode-decode model codes, wrongly, as the receiver simply getting the meaning wrong. I name this gap resolution drift, give it a signed typology (positive drift, where the receiver collapses tighter than the sender meant and manufactures definition that was never sent; negative drift, where the receiver holds looser than the sender meant and loses definition that was), predict from Binary Bias (Temte 2026a) that the unmarked receiver default is positive drift, and locate the decision-theoretic cost of the phenomenon in Quantum-Ethical Decision Algebra (Temte 2025c): the receiver who collapses a cloud the sender meant to leave open is committing a premature collapse on the sender's behalf, on borrowed authority. The framework's highest-stakes arena is human-AI interaction, where both parties drift in characteristic directions. The paper closes with the empirical signature of drift, a set of resolution-marking remedies, and the paper's position in the corpus. It is the production-end sibling, anchored on the sender's intended resolution in live intralinguistic communication, to Translation as Probability Cloud (Temte 2026as) at the reception end, anchored on the recovery of a source meaning across linguistic distance.
references: Grice, H. Paul. (1975). Logic and conversation. In P. Cole and J. Morgan (eds.), Syntax and Semantics, Vol. 3: Speech Acts. New York: Academic Press.
Shannon, Claude E. (1948). A mathematical theory of communication. Bell System Technical Journal, 27, 379-423 and 623-656.
Sperber, Dan, and Deirdre Wilson. (1995). Relevance: Communication and Cognition. 2nd ed. Oxford: Blackwell.

metadata.title: PiereLeonFrederiks/CoreLogger: CoreLogger
metadata.description:
Early access release of the Geoarchaeological Core Logger, a single-file browser tool for logging sediment core and borehole stratigraphy in the field. No installation, no server - runs entirely offline in the browser. This is an early version and subject to change. If you use it in the field, I'd love to hear what works, what's missing, and what could be improved. What's included in this release:

Layer logging with depth, colour, and free-text description Automatic soil classification (DIN/KA5 short codes) Bilingual input: English and German (auto-translation) Munsell colour mapping Layer types: Normal, Disturbed, Sediment gap, Anthropogenic Markers / finds and compaction zone logging GPS capture and manual coordinate entry (WGS84 / projected CRS) Stratigraphic profile canvas with live preview Export: TSV, TXT, JSON, GeODin/SEP-3 CSV, JPG, PNG, SVG, PDF Profile save and re-import (JSON and TXT)

creators.name: Pière Leon Frederiks
